keywords.serpWeakness: Top organic results are brand sites (SunPower, Tesla) and aggregators (EnergySage) that prioritize lead capture over instant accuracy; mobile usability is poor on older calculator tools.
userComplaints: Requires personal information before showing any numbers
Estimates are generic and ignore local utility rates
Doesn't work well on phones – layout broken, sliders unusable

keywords.serpWeakness: Ranking pages are mostly 2-4 years old, using static national average data; none integrate real-time local installation costs or incentives.
userComplaints: Showed $15k but real quotes are $25k+
No way to adjust for roof type or shading

keywords.serpWeakness: Several top results are thin content pages from home service directories (Angi, HomeAdvisor) that redirect to lead forms, leaving an opening for a genuine utility-focused tool.
userComplaints: Just a form to get called back, not an actual calculator

keywords.keyword: how many solar panels do i need calculator
keywords.serpWeakness: Existing calculators assume fixed panel wattage (e.g., 320W) and don't let users adjust for panel efficiency; many are embedded in long-form articles, making the tool less discoverable.
userComplaints: Assumes 300W panels but I want to compare 400W vs 450W
Doesn't ask my roof orientation or tilt

keywords.serpWeakness: Major players (EnergySage, SunPower) have calculators that push toward a quote; opportunity for a no-commitment tool that uses local time-of-use rates and net metering policies.
userComplaints: Savings estimate doesn't match real electric bills
Doesn't include battery savings or grid export

keywords.serpWeakness: Top results are often state-specific energy offices or non-profits with outdated interfaces; commercial sites miss local incentive details, leaving room for a dynamic tool combining federal, state, and utility rebates.
userComplaints: Says payback is 7 years but real-life is 10+ because electricity rates changed
No field for maintenance or inverter replacement costs

keywords.serpWeakness: Many tools are basic kW estimates without roof dimensions; satellite-based sizing tools are buried behind lead forms. A lightweight, map-based sizing tool would stand out.
userComplaints: Have to manually enter roof area – should use satellite data
No option for irregular roof shapes

keywords.serpWeakness: Review articles just list calculators without testing them or comparing outputs; an unbiased, interactive comparison tool where users can test side-by-side would fill the gap.
userComplaints: The 'best' calculator still asked for my email
Reviews don't show actual output differences

keywords.serpWeakness: Few tools explicitly calculate ROI with tax incentives and varying energy price escalation rates; most just give simple payback.
userComplaints: Ignores the time value of money or inflation
Doesn't factor in SREC income

keywords.keyword: are solar panels worth it calculator
keywords.serpWeakness: Results are dominated by Forbes Home, NerdWallet articles that embed a generic lead-gen calculator; a dedicated, more transparent tool could outrank with better user experience and local accuracy.
userComplaints: The NerdWallet calculator asked for contact info before showing savings
Forbes tool was just a glorified lead form

keywords.serpWeakness: Existing tools (PVWatts) are technically accurate but not user-friendly; mobile experience is nearly unusable. A simplified version with interactive maps could attract mobile users.
userComplaints: PVWatts is too complex for a normal homeowner
Can't see monthly output differences on a phone

keywords.serpWeakness: Many top results are apps that require download or advanced inputs; a browser-based, no-signup estimator with immediate results could capture 'near-transactional' traffic.
userComplaints: Tells me to download an app just to get an estimate

keywords.serpWeakness: SERPs show local installer sites with static tables; a dynamic calculator factoring in labor costs by ZIP code and current material prices would be a step ahead.
userComplaints: Prices are from 2022 and don't reflect current labor or panel shortages

keywords.serpWeakness: Top results are aging blog posts from DIY solar sites; missing modern features like electric vehicle charging load or heat pump integration.
userComplaints: Doesn't account for future EV purchase or heat pump

keywords.serpWeakness: Most 'free' calculators end up requiring email to view results; a genuinely free, instant tool would have a powerful unique selling proposition.
userComplaints: It says free but then locks results behind a contact form
